title: Tyler Pavlas -Hiring Manger Interview | Founding Account Executive
meeting_title: Tyler Pavlas -Hiring Manger Interview | Founding Account Executive

transcript.text: And that was when I was in SDR.
I saw that you were connected to Danilo, who was my first boss.
I'm sure you remember him.
He's a guy who's impossible to forget.
That's funny you mention that, because I was literally thinking the same thing earlier.
That guy was very memorable on that trip.
A lot of fun to hang out with.
I mean, you all had an interesting culture back then.
It seemed like you had a good vibe.
People got along really well together.
And, yeah, it was fun to have a long time.
It was definitely the startup culture that, you know, a Texas A&M grad, moving to the Bay right after college, thinking about software.
It was like what I imagined and what I learned is not consistent from company to company.
Like Rainforest, they had their culture figured out.
That was a great place to work.
Wow.
No, I was trying to connect the dots, but I did not realize that you're Clay's husband.
And we met in Cabo, apparently.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
Yeah.
Culture is a funny thing, and I've always been fascinated.
Like, when I first moved to the Bay 13 years ago, I was already, like, you know, five and a half, six years into my career or so.
Only one company previous to the Bay.
And anyways, like, first company, I was just, like, super fortunate, like, amazing culture.
And I remember Rainforest kind of, like, reminded me of that the way Claire would describe it and just seeing how y'all got along and this and that.
And so it's kind of fascinated me of, like, how do you create those cultures?
How do you, like, you know, maintain them?
It's way easier said than done.
But you can just kind of, like, feel it and see it when you're a part of that or you're around people that are a part of that culture.
And I don't know, maybe I only saw a sliver of and it wasn't that, but at least that was my interpretation is it seemed like y'all really enjoyed working together and it was a lot of fun.
And I'm sure there were some ups and downs naturally come with a startup of that phase.
But, yeah, whenever I've been at a couple of companies that have had that and.
It's, yeah, it makes all the hard work a lot more fun.
Absolutely.
mean, at Rainforest, part of it was you had to make crowdsource QA fun.
So they had a great culture in office to keep us motivated.

And then I think as well, if you can humanize yourself at work or make that part of the culture from the top, it just makes it more fun for people to wake up every day and work really hard, knowing that they're doing it alongside people they like.
They know more than just their work version of their self.
When there's a real opportunity, that's also what's conducive to a great culture.
Like Segment, when I was there, we were chasing IPO or a huge exit.
We got there.
That's part of what made it super fun is, you know, you're showing up with something that can really make an impact, right, through what you put in it.
So, it's tough in a remote environment, but I have no doubt about the opportunity at GrowthX, and I'm sure that fuels, right, the team each day when you all.
Yeah, totally.
And you're on the East Coast now, right?
Houston, Texas.
So I came back home.
My fiance, she's born and raised in Houston as well.
We're just close to our parents.
Nice, nice.
Very cool.
So yeah, not very, what is that, central time?
So I'm not too far away, but I mean, tech world always reminds me, it is quite small.
I feel like I have some little reminder of that each and every month in some ways, someone I meet in some sense or another.
So yeah, cool.
The small world, man.
Good to reconnect.
I think you got a little background on me.
I watched a bit of your call with Bridget, or vast majority of that call, but happy to just like start there quick.
So just get a good intro, or did you already get a pretty good overview?
Yeah, mean, I'd love to hear like...
like...
In your words, kind of how your role differs from Jason's.
And I'd also love to hear a little bit of a story of just being one of the first customers at home base to joining full time.
I didn't get too much of an intro on you, so I'd love to hear some more of that.
Yeah, cool, cool.
So, yeah, I've worn a variety of different hats here, like, you know, helping lead on the sales side, but not say I'm like a very clearly like not my background is not in sales.
And, you know, I joined back in October, you know, helping out in a variety of different places.
The need largely was to help ensure, like, we were setting our active clients up to success.
And just because I have an agency background as well as in-house and was the first customer, it just like was a nice kind of like blend to leading and helping scale on the client services side.
So a of that was like a combination of like making sure clients were happy.
You know, we were driving.
As well as, like, we were building the right team and enabling that team for success.
And, you know, a lot of fun, a lot of challenges along the way.
were just like, especially like December onwards, we were just like growing so fast and a lot of learning from that.
Like, you know, we got into this rhythm where, you know, we were hiring, probably not giving people enough onboarding time and then like getting straight into like managing accounts too quickly.
And so that's why, you know, we pivoted into some of the things Bridget shared on like strategy sprints.
And we feel really good actually about that model now and how it all works and how we'll better enable the team for success.
And just varying things we've like, experiments we've learned from like team structure.
So it was part of like some of the early phases of like bringing that together.
And then, you know, there was just a need on the sales side.
And we felt that like, hey, you know, given the way we sell is kind of unique.
Like, you know, Marcel, for example, being some.
With a somewhat similar background, like his background skews like heavier content than mine probably does, but just in that like more like growth generalists in a sense.
And so like we've done the role of most of the people we sell to.

And so the mindset was like, hey, you know, can I help us keep momentum by having that, you know, having some, buying some trust of like, hey, like you both like saw it from the early days, like we're the first customer.
You have got to see across all the customers and now like more consultative approach of like, you know, just trying to talk about like what's going to be the right fit for them strategically.
And like, does this make sense for them or not?
And what would that look like?
And I'd say probably there's aspects of our selling motion is probably a little unique as a result for better or worse.
But yeah, anyways, previous to that long career in digital and growth, 19 years, variety, different stops.
Most of my experience is just like companies, like they learn at one point or another.
That they spend too much on paid search, or they did that at a previous company, and they want to avoid those pitfalls, and they really need to diversify their channels.
And so a lot of times what I was hired at varying stops was to help them with that.
Like, how do you stand up these other channels where paid search is oftentimes one of the easiest places for companies to start?

Because it's like, you pick some keywords, you just go spend a bunch of money on ads, you're demand capturing, you're capturing a lot of what's out there, and you're trying to, like, get those feeds into your SDRs, et cetera, or whatever your model is.
So it can work for fast success, but, like, then, you know, past handful of years, CAC pressures that a lot of businesses have been feeling, you know, capitalism.
It's a little bit easier to get now in the past year or so, but especially it was, like, quite difficult for a period of time there.
And so, you know, things like standing up other channels and organic and spinning up those channels is a big focus, and that's a lot of times why I was hired.

So when I was at Homebase, that was a big reason they brought me on was they were spending just a ton of money, like half a million a month in paid search and like getting a lot of signups, like, you know, variety of different things, inflating the cost of that and hard to like, there's just a lot that's like out of your control when you're too relying on that.
And so anyways, long story short, that's why I was brought on there, met Marcel when he was doing his workshops.
I'm like, oh, , this is like building on some pretty cool stuff.
I used to do a track, but now like in a whole other level because now you have AI and LLMs in the mix.
And then I was like, , man, I don't have time to like build all this.
Like I'm already signed up for all these OKRs.
My team doesn't have time for this.
Like, can you please like help build this for me?
And it just kind of all grew from there.
I joke with Marcel, like I did not join because you all were just like crushing it so hard for what we were doing at Homebase.
was a lot of messy things and actually being the first customer and figuring that out.
It was.

And what has brought me here today is just so much opportunity and what we can grow into and just this unique place where so many go-to-market teams that have pressures of how to bring AI in some form or fashion into what they do.
They don't know where to start.
And what that means and what we provide as a service, obviously there's very specific things we do today.
I think there's some interesting angles for us to explore even in the future beyond that.
But anyways, that's a good one all day about that, but that's not the intention for the convo today.
But yeah, it's a little bit of background.
Yeah, that helps a lot, Kyle.

guess like a really direct question in your role right now as VP of customer growth, are you more focused on like net new client acquisition or systematizing the process with new clients that come in or upselling and renewing the clients?
Or, you know, it's probably all the above, right?
But I guess what's the main focus for you right now of those three?
Right now it's.
Even the, it's like landing new logos.
So like largely doing the role of an AE, like having those intro calls, follow-up calls, bringing them ourselves when I need to.
You know, making sure I'm doing the right handoffs for the internal team to enable them for success, et cetera.
You know, there naturally would be other things I'll get pulled into as we start to bring in other folks who can kind of like take that on.
Because, you know, for example, those strategy sprints that, you know, we do today, you know, we really have like a handful of people who can like lead those teams.
And that's going to continue to grow with like all the customers that are in strategy sprints.
You know, they'll graduate into execution, the execution phase into the delivery team.
But, you know, as we, today we try to limit to two kickoffs a week.
Eventually we feel like we need to get to three in the not too distant future.
Maybe there's a future this year.
I'm not sure.
We haven't clicked.
Really said that for sure is where it's for.
So likely will help diving into that as well and running some of those strategy sprints.
so probably, you know, naturally just going to be wearing multiple hats and helping out where I can and whatever the pain is, just, you know, kind of the nature of startups.
so where the differentiation to your question for like Jason, he's more focused on like he's helping a little bit on the delivery side because he has a deep background on like he's quite technical.
And helping us refine what we deliver.
But his core focus of his role is really about helping us feed the top of the funnel.
So he's helping us spin up.
We just launched our own publication.
We call it The Output.
We're going to have a podcast.
We're literally recording today with hyper growth guys.
don't know if you're familiar with them, but G and Gonto, these guys are ridiculously legit.
Actually, the former had to grow that segment.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I actually worked with.
G to bring Segment to G2 Crowd, so I actually met G as well.
It's cool.
I've been following him for most of my career.
He's crazy legit, pretty much like that G in growth.
No pun intended.
But anyways, so Jason's role was doing things like that, helping us spin up and feed the top of the funnel, help us get things like workshops out there, et cetera.
And so, you know, some things where there's some like overlap and we both can like step in, but that's kind of like just actual startup stuff.
Yeah, it makes sense.
I mean, I'm excited about the founding AE role for a lot of reasons that I want to get into with you.
I guess it sounds like for this founding AE, you're hoping to have someone that can come in and really autonomously like close deals as part of the process that has worked so far.
So that you can free up more time to think about how to be successful.
How is this?
How do we scale a lot of these new clients and make them successful?
Is that accurate that you would be doing like less of the net new work with this founding aid taking a lot taking on a lot of the I would say that's relatively accurate.
There's probably times where like I'll be like, you know, you know, running some deals depending on like capacity and like what's needed and things of that nature.
And like but largely, yes, I would say that's that's accurate.
And the the the slight tweak, I would say, to that framing is also hopefully someone who can help us like collaborate and build that as well.
Some of those systems today, like we have like a relatively like rough playbook.
Right.
So we have, you know, we're kind of doing this like hybrid a bit where we're trying to not lengthen our process.
Intentionally, but there can be some we.
We need to rethink that and adapt it.
And I would love to have a partner who wants to experiment with that and can just think through, like, hey, here's how I've done this before.
Like, I understand why we're doing it this way.
But I think if we do it this way, like, let's try this out and see if this works better.
So as an example, like we, you know, we have this kind of like hybrid of like how we approach like discovery in our intros and then kind of like digging in deeper.
Well, we're kind of essentially in the intro, we're trying to qualify and do some light discovery.
And then to our follow up meeting, we're kind of if there's other areas of discovery, we need to dive in deeper.
We're trying to do that.

We're also trying to like start to like inspire and do some light solutioning and talk about like, hey, this is, you know, this is some things we've done for similar use cases that could apply to you.
Or here's some opportunities we see.
Get them excited about like, hey, like.
Some of them, it's just like, oh, my God, like, have massive opportunity in terms of, like, if you just start to tackle these things, like, you have so much growth potential.
Some others, it's not as obvious, and we have to kind of, like, you know, dig in deeper and make sure we understand.
So anyways, point being is, like, we don't have, like, a textbook, like, this is only a pure discovery call, like, step, right?
And maybe that's right, maybe that's wrong, et cetera.
But usually what we're trying to do is, like, have that intro, have that follow-up.
It's not rare for us to close deals after two meetings, maybe three.
But we're certainly starting to see some of those that are going to be linked, naturally so for a variety of reasons.
Part of that is, like, I think that has to be the expectation to a degree of, like, moving beyond just pure founder-led sales.
Because, you know, like, over time, we're going to exhaust Mars' network to some degree or another.
He's not going to have as much of a direct connection with these folks.
And so it's going to take them a little bit.
let's Let's
Or to be convinced and steps that we're going to have to go through.
And so as you do that, what are some of the things that are necessary to support that?
And so we're early phases of that journey, right?
Of adding some elements that support that, but definitely a lot more that needs to be done.
And so the challenge has been for me to have the capacity to address that.
Largely just making sure we have things coming in and we're closing deals.
And then like, you know, trying to find some scrappy ways to move fast, essentially.
So love a partner to just like help build that and someone who's like excited.
Obviously, there's a balancing game, right?
Like you can't pull someone too much into that.
And then they're just not able to actually focus on the core aspect of closing deals.
I totally get that.
But hopefully there are some things to address that by being a little bit like medium term focus makes their job much easier to enable them to get to a point where they're closing.
Yeah, for sure.
I mean, it really resonates like the sales process.
Justin, where it's at right now, it matches like the style of how the company goes to market, right?
It's outcomes as opposed to software that you're selling.
Software could come once you figure out like what are those repeatable solutions that customers need that we can deliver results around.
And so, you know, I think the things that resonate for me with the role, but also with GrowthX, one, the way you're going to market, I don't see anybody doing this, right?
This idea of outcomes and almost an agency type of solution, but within the framework of how a traditional software company would build their business.
You know, you have a bigger TAM because of that too, right?
You can go after services, which is a lot bigger than software.
You've got Marcel and his name recognition and the outcomes that have already been delivered that you can use as a seller.
That's why you're closing meetings and, you know, two calls is you're able to say like, here's what we did for this client.
And you speak to the pain points that software.
Right?
Like the team could go and buy the software, but then they have to learn how to use it.
Then it's on them to create the content.
Okay, they could go leverage the AI, but do they have the expertise to actually get the posts out of the AI frameworks?
And so it's like this in between the value prop of agencies are too narrow and expensive that you're selling in.
And absolutely to your point, like there's a need to do both.
Close the deals, but then figure out how to do it in a repeatable way.
Figure out how to maybe slow down to speed up so that the client follows more of a consistent framework.
And that you're not, right, reinventing the wheel or adding a new service each time a client comes in.
I mean, there's so much we could go into here.
So I won't let you guide me, but I feel like...
It's a great framing.
It's a great framing.
I very much agree.
I think it's an interesting place to be.
You kind of nailed it of like, I think more and more business...
It's like buying software.
There's always going to be some aspect of that, but that is going to become a rare, rare thing as you have things that can just solve multiple solutions.
And it'll be interesting for us to like, how, why do we go with that?
It's different things Marcel and I have talked about over time.
Like, for example, like just different things in running business that we've spun up in terms of workflows.
And we're just like, those are like whole companies that just like do that thing that we just like built.
And like, it's pretty badass.
And like, is this something that we offer one day?
And like, one of the examples are, you know, what we're doing all about hiring.
I was mentioning of ramping up the client service side of the business.
We didn't have any recruiters.
We literally like had this whole process of like inbound applicants and asking for a bit more upfront.
won't go too in depth on that, but you see some of the aspects of it today.
It's like hire flicks.
Like, you know, I think you might've gone through that a little bit, although we outbound it too, I believe.
So it's a little different.
But anyways, automating, synthesizing, parsing those resumes out.
And the answer.

And like flagging who's a fit and who's not, and then going through like a variety of other steps and then just like building these sorts of solutions that, again, like literally there's tools that just do that.

And so it's just, there's going to be more and more of these things that exist out there and it doesn't make sense to just like purely build like these very use case restrictive one-off things as much and more, you know, wider services that are going to focus on like, yeah, wider outcomes you're after and like, how do you customize towards that?
And so I think we're still trying to figure that out in a lot of ways because, you know, that's quite broad.
And like, as Bridget shared, like we found at times you go to your custom that can be like, that can not set us up for success, even though, you know, we've worked.
Sometimes we have to, we have to like be careful of like our excitement and our passion.
Like,  yeah, we can build all this cool stuff.
It's going to be cool.
And then we get into it and we're like, oh my God, this looks so much to figure out and define and core.
Anyways, I think the point is you need AI to create content at this scale and you need expertise to leverage the AI effectively.
And what I've learned selling Seros is that, you know, there's a quality play to the content as well.
And I feel like your model, it addresses all three, right?

You're not going into distribution and conversion until you've figured out, well, what's our messaging and how do we create this content engine and what kind of content is going to be created as part of that work.
But when I try to sell Seros, you know, we're putting all the onus on the graphic design team who's already swamped.
And then the marketing side, helping us build a business case where there can be a line item for interactive content where there's just not.
But where is there always a line item, right?
There's always a line item with paid ads that's huge.
And there's always a line item for...
agencies or freelancers.

And so that's why everything clicked after Tucker reached out to me was, oh, wait, I mean, I've done this before being the founding AE at AIR and building a product that wasn't delivering outcomes today.
It was just solving for a lot of pain points and was cool to use.
And I've also been on the opposite end of the spectrum where we sell outcomes here at Seros, but it's on the bonus of the customer to create the content.

And so to me, GrowthX was like, well, I could do AER again, but now I'm selling outcomes, which I feel like I've gotten pretty dangerous at with Seros because you're connecting animated content to hitting their lead gen targets or increasing their win rates.
You know, on the buying end, I kind of see GrowthX as like a no brainer.
And to your point, it's just a matter of getting to outcomes quickly, starting with why, and then scale, like selling in a way that's scalable.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Thank
I've seen that at AIR, where we would sell things that maybe weren't a core part of where we wanted our product to go.
And it would hurt us in the long run if we figured out how to manage all that technical debt and connect this feature to where we're ultimately trying to take the product.
And one of the reasons I left AIR was because it became more of a product-led business than an enterprise sales business.
And, you know, I didn't see our product reaching the place that, like, an NBCUniversal would need it to get to, where it's automated and working in tandem with their content management system.
As soon as they're making a status change, other systems get updated.
AIR was never that.
It was just a better way of using Dropbox or Google Drops, you know.
Interesting.
Yeah, I was just going to ask if I recall you giving a quick on it, but I'm now struggling to remember what exactly AIR does.
Yeah, give me a quick refresh on that.
Yeah, so when I was at AIR, we were branding ourselves as a creative operations platform.
But we were selling against digital asset management systems.

So that was part of the problem on never getting to an enterprise product is you're trying to develop creative operations functionality and all of these like random pieces of product functionality that a dam would need at the enterprise level.
And so like what was there?
was a home for all of your media.
And it was a place where you could organize it more seamlessly, research more seamlessly.
And that's creative operations a little bit because we would be like uploading versions and using a desktop sync and approving assets.
It just became like a lot of features to build for when the enterprise side, they were really looking for like a less clunky dam.
They were looking for something that was going to be much more user friendly than a binder.
And error was that, but it didn't have like all the integrations that an enterprise might need.
Interesting.
Okay.
Yeah, I could see how that'd be a bit of a...
So many businesses trying to figure out that angle of where does enterprise and PLG sit alongside.
When I was at Trey, it was a very similar thing.
It was like pure, typical lead gen sales, base sales.
And then they started bringing in PLG and had some success, but just a ton of pain in learning along the way of how to have both those motions work alongside each other.
And just this idea of how do you get the right people in the funnel and try it and make sure you're getting that right momentum with enterprises is a tricky game on the PLG side.
It's very doable.
It just very much necessitates you having to change a lot of different things in your go-to-market motion.
So, yeah, I could see that creating some headaches on the sales side for sure.
I mean, it's great for lead flow, right?
You've got all these three users that you can go after, but to your point, like, how do you bring an enterprise plan?
And or solution alongside something that you could pay, you know, a couple of 10 or a hundred bucks a month to get access to.
And that's really what I joined AIR to do was create that enterprise offering with our CEO and then figure out how to land enterprise logos.
And, you know, I ended up leaving because I just got to a place where I realized, hey, what these enterprise customers want, like we're not going to ship anytime soon.
And I think I'd rather sell something that is real and connect to outcomes versus something that designers and marketers really love using.
But AIR's leaned into that and it's worked for them, which is great.
I think it's a little more fun on the sales side when you can really work backwards from what's the business goal that you're trying to drive to?
Why can't you get there today?
And what can our software solution do to get you there faster and better than the alternatives?
Yeah, I love it.
I'm trying to remember which business.

We're referring to this, I recall you mentioning something in your combo with Bridget, maybe it was at Seros, where you said something to the effect of running outbound via your CEOs like LinkedIn and managing messages through that.
Was that at Seros?
No, at Seros, I got SDR support.
It was at Air.
It was like, me and Shane, the CEO, hey, how are we going to start going after some of our target accounts that could be enterprise customers at Air?
Or, you know, well, you're pretty popular on LinkedIn.
Why don't we just reach out to the creative directors across all these companies and basically send a message that's along the lines of interviewing them?
And we'll ask them what creative operations means to them, how the field has evolved over time and their careers.
And then we'll plant some seeds about how Air is a platform for creative operations.
And that's, you know, how we got some of our best clients like NBCUniversal came from that LinkedIn play.
Believe it or not.
Oh, pretty cool.
So just to, can you walk me through like how you built the process a little bit just to make sure I understand it?
Like were you ghostwriting those messages for them?
Were you helping them identify who to read that to?
Like all that, that work.
Yeah.
So, I mean, the first thing that we did was we like basically decided what our target industries were, who our target accounts were in those industries.
We pulled a lot of reports of accounts that we could go after and then manually, right, grabbed all the creative directors from those accounts.
And then I had access to his LinkedIn where, you know, we created a message together and it was rinse and repeat.
know, pretty much would use the same message on most people we reached out to.

Maybe if it was a really good one, you know, we'd add one sentence of personalization and we'd have like a three-step process where we would get a response from, you know, least a quarter, not half, but close to it because people.
Well, you know, resonated with the CEO of a cool creative software company, reaching out to them, right, individually to get their perspective on creative operations.
So reporting and CRMs into a tactical login to the LinkedIn profile and a lot of messaging.
Okay, cool, cool.
well, think one of the interesting aspects, you know, obviously, so much has changed now with where AI can sit in the mix.
They can support just about any role.
Obviously, we look at ourselves very much as, like, how do we make our team AI native and, like, really value that.
So I'm curious, like, what are some of, like, the things you're leveraging today with AI?
Like, what's been working?
What hasn't?
What are you excited about?
How, know, how has your approach evolved?
I'd love to, yeah, hear a little bit about that.
Yeah, great question.
For me, I would say I'm trying to think about AI in places where I spend the most time.
And where I spend the most time is typically trying to catch myself up to speed on an opportunity when I've got 50, right, that I might be working on and they're all in different stages.
So what I do is I will actually open up threads with ChatGPT and basically add the transcripts from Gong to those threads.
And I'll ask it very specific questions, right?
Based on this transcript, you know, one to 10, how likely would this deal be closed in this quarter, next quarter?

Or more simply, hey, summarize all these transcripts and give me the top five pain points, top five use cases, and any specific examples that resonated since we're sharing a lot of content with Sarah.
Maybe I've got a big call coming up.
Hey, here's everything I've talked about with this customer.
Give me the 10.
It's important questions that I should ask if I was working to close this sales opportunity.
It's really just a lot of take it out of my head, put it into the system, and then use those insights to be faster, like with my creativity.
It's not trying to start from zero and then come up with, right, one to 10 examples.
It's feeding it to AI and basically having it come up with the examples and then me taking it to the next step from there.
So those are, like, the main use cases, notes consolidation, prep before an opportunity, reality check on how real the opportunity is based on the transcript.
Not much more beyond that.
Cool, cool.
Yeah, no, definitely not a fool.
What is, I mean, generally, how have you found that?
Like, are there things you've realized that are just, you have to tweak your approach to make it more helpful?
Or, oh, , this is just not going to work and I can't rely on AI to help me here.
I'd love to hear about that as well.
I think, I mean, one thing that I think is very helpful is providing a lot of context.

### Sales Process and Scaling

  - [Currently doing 2-3 kickoffs per week, aiming to increase to 5-10](https://fathom.video/share/tcR7HxjYxWM-Q8mLpt1fY_pE-5FvjTyG?tab=summary&timestamp=654.0)

### AI Integration in Sales

  - [Tyler uses AI for notes consolidation, opportunity prep, and reality checks](https://fathom.video/share/tcR7HxjYxWM-Q8mLpt1fY_pE-5FvjTyG?tab=summary&timestamp=1800.0)
